goal_title_suggestions: Launch my SaaS to first paying users
Build and ship a SaaS product
Go from idea to paying SaaS customers

variants: Open a notes app right now and write down 3 specific problems you've personally experienced that frustrated you enough to pay for a fix.
Pull up your phone's notes app and list 3 real problems you've faced — not hypothetical ones, problems that cost you time or money.
Write 3 problems you've personally hit in the last 12 months where you thought 'why doesn't a tool exist for this' — put them in a note now.
tasks.why: Your best SaaS idea lives in your own frustrations — not in trend reports.

variants: For each of your 3 problems, write one sentence on who else likely has this problem and whether they'd pay to solve it — do this in the same note.
Under each problem in your list, add a quick answer to: who else suffers from this and does money change hands when they try to fix it today?
Annotate each of your 3 problems with two things: the type of person who has it and whether a solution exists that costs money right now.
tasks.why: Problems worth building on have a market — you need to verify that before picking one.

variants: Read all 3 problems again, pick the ONE you understand most deeply and have the strongest gut pull toward, circle it, and write one sentence on why you chose it over the others.
Score each problem 1-5 on two criteria — personal expertise and market obviousness — then pick the highest scorer as your focus problem and write down the score.
Compare your 3 problems side by side: which one do you have the most personal context on? Pick that one, write 'CHOSEN:' next to it, and delete the others from your note.
tasks.why: You need ONE problem to validate — splitting focus kills early-stage momentum.

variants: Open your note from yesterday, read your chosen problem, and write a single crisp sentence that describes it: who has it, what it costs them, and when it happens.
Take yesterday's chosen problem and compress it into one sentence using this format: '[Person] struggles with [problem] when [situation] and it costs them [time/money/frustration].'
Rewrite your chosen problem as if you're texting a friend — one sentence, plain English, no jargon — so they immediately understand the pain.
tasks.why: A sharp problem statement is the foundation of every pitch, landing page, and DM you'll send.

variants: Search Reddit for your problem right now — find 2 posts where people are complaining about it and copy the exact phrases they use into your note.
Go to Reddit, search for your problem using 3 different keyword combinations, and paste 2 real complaints you find into your notes with the subreddit names.
Open Reddit and find 2 threads where your target user describes this exact pain — copy their words verbatim into your problem note, source included.
tasks.why: Real user language from Reddit is your future landing page copy and DM opener.

variants: Write a list of 10 specific people — by name, handle, or job title — who have your problem, then identify 3 you could actually reach out to this week.
Build a list of 10 potential users for your problem: name, where you'd find them online, and one reason they'd take your DM — put this in a new note called 'Outreach Targets'.
Create your first outreach target list: 10 people who have your chosen problem, sourced from LinkedIn, Reddit, Twitter, or your existing network — include how you'd contact each one.
tasks.why: You need 10 conversations to validate. Building the list now means you start messaging tomorrow.

variants: Write a DM template — 3 sentences max — that explains who you are, what problem you're exploring, and asks one specific question about how they currently handle it.
Draft your outreach message right now: one sentence on who you are, one on the problem you're researching, one question asking how they solve it today — keep it under 60 words.
Write the DM you'll send to potential users this week: short intro, name the problem, ask 'how do you currently handle X?' — no pitching, no product mention, under 3 sentences.
tasks.why: A pre-written message means you send it — improvising means you procrastinate.

variants: Send your first DM to 3 people from your outreach list using your template — personalize the opener for each one with one specific detail about them.
Pick the top 3 people from yesterday's list, customize your message slightly for each, and send all 3 DMs right now — don't edit them for more than 30 seconds each.
Fire off 3 outreach messages today: use your template, tweak the first sentence for each person, and hit send on all 3 before you close this app.
tasks.why: 3 conversations started today beats a perfect message never sent.

variants: Open a Google Doc or Notion page titled 'Validation Log' and create a table with columns: Name, Date, Key Quote, and Insight — leave it ready for entries.
Set up your validation tracker now: a simple spreadsheet or Notion table with Person, Date Contacted, Response (Y/N), and their exact words about the problem.
Create a 'Customer Discovery' doc with a table to log every conversation — columns for who you talked to, what they said about the problem, and your key takeaway — add today's 3 as pending.
tasks.why: Pattern recognition across 10 conversations only works if you track them systematically.

variants: Check your DMs right now and log any responses from yesterday's outreach in your Validation Log — even 'not interested' counts.
Open your messages, read any replies to your day-3 DMs, and update your Validation Log with the response status for each person.
Pull up yesterday's 3 outreach messages, note who replied and who didn't in your tracker, and mark the status for each.
tasks.why: Tracking responses tells you your open rate — which you'll need to optimize.

variants: Send 3 more DMs from your outreach list — these should be to slightly different types of people than day 3 to test if the problem spans multiple sub-groups.
Pick 3 more targets from your list and send them your outreach message today — aim for people in different roles or contexts than your first batch.
Fire another 3 DMs from your outreach list — vary the recipients: different industries, different seniority levels, or different contexts where your problem shows up.
tasks.why: Breadth of early responses shows you which user type has the sharpest pain.

variants: Search IndieHackers for your problem space and find 2 products already in the market — write down their pricing and one thing their reviews say is missing.
Do a quick competitor scan: Google your problem + 'software' or 'tool', find 2 existing solutions, and note their price and top 1-star complaint.
Look up 2 existing tools that address your problem on ProductHunt or IndieHackers and note: their price, their positioning, and the gap their users complain about most.
tasks.why: Competition confirms the market exists — and gaps tell you where to position.

variants: Check your Validation Log and count total DMs sent vs. replies received — write the ratio at the top of the doc.
Open your tracker, tally your outreach stats: X sent, Y replied, Z conversations started — write those 3 numbers prominently in the doc.
Pull up your Validation Log and calculate your response rate so far — write it as a fraction (e.g., 2/6) at the top of the page.
tasks.why: Knowing your response rate tells you if your message or targeting needs adjusting.

variants: If you got any replies, respond to each one with a follow-up question: 'How often does this happen to you, and what do you do right now to deal with it?'
Reply to every DM response you've gotten with this exact follow-up: 'How do you currently handle [problem] — what's your workaround?'
Send follow-ups to anyone who replied: ask them to describe their current workaround for the problem in one or two sentences — keep the convo going.
tasks.why: The second question reveals whether the pain is acute or just a mild annoyance.

variants: Write a 'Problem Brief' in your notes: 2-3 sentences describing the problem, who has it, how they solve it today, and what the solution costs them in time or money.
Compile everything you know so far into a one-paragraph problem brief: the target user, the pain, the current workaround, and the opportunity — write it fresh from memory.
Draft a problem brief document pulling from your Validation Log and competitor research: define the problem, the person, the current fix they use, and why that fix falls short.
tasks.why: A written brief forces clarity — if you can't write it, you don't understand it yet.

variants: Send 2 more DMs from your outreach list — you should be at 8 total by end of today.
Fire 2 more outreach messages from your target list — get your sent count to 8 before the day ends.
Pick 2 more names from your list and send the DMs right now — you need 10 total conversations started by end of day 14.
tasks.why: Consistent daily outreach compounds — 2 per day for a week is your validation sample.

variants: Search Twitter/X for your problem using 3 keywords and find 3 people who complained about it in the last 30 days — add them to your outreach list.
Go to Twitter/X, search the exact phrases people use for your problem (use terms from your Reddit research), and add 3 relevant accounts to your target list.
Use Twitter/X advanced search with your problem keywords, find 3 users who've posted about this pain in the past month, and add them as priority outreach targets.
tasks.why: Twitter/X is a live complaint board — people who post about problems are primed to talk to you.

variants: Write down the #1 question you don't yet have an answer to about your target user — then find one source online (blog, Reddit, YouTube video title) that might answer it and note what you learned.
Identify the biggest gap in your understanding of the user's problem, find one online source that addresses it, and add a key insight to your Problem Brief.
Look at your Problem Brief and find the weakest assumption in it — then spend 8 minutes looking for evidence that either confirms or challenges that assumption online.
tasks.why: Validation means testing assumptions — not just collecting confirmations.

variants: Open your Validation Log and review every outreach attempt from this week — count total sent, total replies, and write 3 bullet points on what you've learned so far.
Do a week-1 review in your Validation Log: list every person you contacted, their response, and your single biggest insight from the conversations so far.
Review your entire first week of outreach: check your tracker, note total DMs sent vs. conversations had, and write down the most surprising thing you heard from a real user.
tasks.why: Week reviews catch drift early — you need to know if your outreach is working before week 2.

variants: Based on your week-1 responses, rewrite your DM template — adjust the opening line or question to reflect what's resonating or what's falling flat.
Update your outreach message using what you learned this week — if responses were low, change the subject line or opening; if they were high, keep what's working and tighten the question.
Revise your DM template now: compare your sent messages to the ones that got replies and update the version for week 2 to reflect what actually landed.
tasks.why: Your outreach message is a hypothesis — week 1 data tells you whether it's working.

variants: Send a follow-up to every person who replied this week but hasn't scheduled a call yet — ask if they'd do a 10-minute voice call or Loom exchange about the problem.
Reach back out to your best conversations from this week and ask directly: 'Would you do a 10-min call or voice note about this? I'm building something and want to hear your experience.'
Message everyone who engaged with you this week and invite them to a quick 10-minute conversation — offer to do it async via Loom if they're short on time.
tasks.why: A real conversation tells you 10x more than a text reply — push for the call.

variants: Send 2 new DMs using your updated week-2 template — these should be fresh contacts, not people you've already messaged.
Fire 2 more outreach messages with your revised template to new contacts from your target list.
Pick 2 people you haven't messaged yet and send your updated DM to them right now.
tasks.why: Consistent outreach momentum keeps your pipeline full while conversations deepen.

variants: If you have a call scheduled, write 5 specific questions you'll ask — focused on their current behavior, not your solution idea.
Prepare 5 interview questions for your first user conversation: ask about what they do today, how often the problem occurs, and what they've already tried — zero mention of your product.
Draft your user interview script: 5 open-ended questions about the problem — when it happens, how they cope, what they've tried, and what a fix would be worth to them.
tasks.why: Prepared questions prevent you from pitching instead of listening.

variants: Log your first real user insight: write the exact quote from a reply or conversation that confirmed or challenged your problem assumption, then write what it means for your direction.
Add your most valuable piece of user evidence to your Validation Log — exact words, source, and a one-line interpretation of what it means for your product direction.
Find the strongest signal you've gotten so far from any user interaction and write it up in your log: what they said, where the conversation happened, and what you'll do differently because of it.
tasks.why: Explicit insight logging builds the case for your chosen problem — or flags a pivot early.

variants: Send 3 DMs today — you should be hitting 13+ total outreach attempts by now, so dig into secondary contacts if your primary list is thinning.
Fire 3 more outreach messages — if your original list of 10 is nearly exhausted, pull in 3 new targets from Reddit, LinkedIn, or Twitter before sending.
Send 3 DMs today: check your tracker, see who's left on your list, and if you need more contacts, find them on the platform where your target user is most active.
tasks.why: 14 conversations by day 14 is the goal — staying on pace matters.

variants: If you've had any user conversations, write a summary in your Validation Log: what problem framing did they use, what do they currently pay or do to solve it, and do they want a better solution?
Compile insights from any conversations you've had so far — write answers to three questions: How do users describe the problem in their own words? What are they paying now? Would they switch?
Add a conversation summary to your Validation Log for each person you've spoken to: their exact words about the problem, their current solution, and their interest level in a new tool.
tasks.why: Patterns across multiple conversations are your product brief — you need them written down.

variants: Based on your conversations so far, write a one-paragraph draft of your value proposition: who it's for, what problem it solves, and why the existing options fall short.
Draft your first value proposition sentence using real language from your user conversations — avoid describing features, describe the outcome the user gets.
Write a value proposition statement using the template 'We help [who] do [outcome] without [pain they hate]' — use the exact words your users have used in conversations.
tasks.why: A value prop built from real user language converts better than one built from assumptions.

variants: Check your DMs for new replies and update your Validation Log — note any new quotes or insights from responses that came in overnight.
Open all your outreach channels right now, log any new replies in your tracker, and add any useful quotes to your Problem Brief.
Do a quick response check: scan all your DMs, update your Validation Log with new replies, and flag any contacts who look ready for a deeper conversation.
tasks.why: Staying on top of replies keeps conversations warm — a 48-hour gap kills momentum.

variants: Post in one online community today — Reddit, IndieHackers, or a Slack/Discord — a question about how people deal with your target problem, without mentioning your product.
Drop a genuine question post in a relevant Reddit or IndieHackers community asking how people handle your problem — frame it as curiosity, not a product pitch.
Find one active online community where your target users hang out and post a question about the problem you're solving — purely curious framing, no product mention.
tasks.why: Community posts generate multiple data points at once and surface people to DM.

variants: Review the competitor tools you found on day 4 — read 5 of their 1-star reviews and write down the exact complaint pattern in your notes.
Go back to the 2 competitor tools from your day-4 research and read their negative reviews on G2, Capterra, or the App Store — list the top 3 complaints you see more than once.
Pull up the competitor products you researched earlier and mine their worst reviews for patterns — write down the 2-3 complaints that appear in multiple reviews.
tasks.why: Competitor complaints are unmet needs — that's your product's competitive wedge.

variants: Send 2 DMs to the new contacts you found from yesterday's community post — these are warm leads since they engaged with your question.
Message 2 people who responded to or engaged with your community post yesterday — they've already shown interest in the problem.
Reach out to the most engaged responders from yesterday's community post — send them a DM thanking them and asking if you can ask 2 more questions.
tasks.why: People who respond to problem posts are self-qualifying — they're your best early leads.

variants: Look at your Validation Log and count how many people have confirmed the problem is real vs. those who were lukewarm — write the ratio and note what the lukewarm ones said differently.
Audit your conversations so far: categorize each response as 'strong pain', 'mild pain', or 'no pain' and count how many fall in each bucket.
Review every conversation in your Validation Log and tag each one: does this person have an acute version of the problem or a mild one? Count each tag and write the result.
tasks.why: You want the acute-pain users — they're who you build for and who will pay first.

variants: Write a 'Solution Sketch' — not a feature list, but a one-paragraph description of what the ideal tool would do for your target user based on what they've told you.
Based on your conversations so far, write one paragraph describing the tool your users actually want — describe it in terms of outcomes and time saved, not features and screens.
Draft a solution sketch in your notes: given everything users have told you, describe in plain language what a perfect tool would let them do in under 5 minutes per day.
tasks.why: A solution sketch grounded in user language is your MVP specification.

variants: Send 2 more DMs — you should be near or past 15 total outreach attempts by now.
Fire 2 more outreach messages to bring your running total to 15+ contacts.
Send today's 2 DMs from your target list — track them in your Validation Log as always.
tasks.why: Hitting 10+ conversations by day 14 is your validation checkpoint.

variants: Look at your solution sketch from yesterday and write down the single most essential action the user needs to be able to do — the one thing without which the tool is useless.
Identify the core action your tool must enable: from your solution sketch, what is the one thing a user should be able to do in 60 seconds that solves 80% of the pain?
Read your solution sketch and circle the most critical capability — then write 'MVP = just this' at the top of the document.
tasks.why: Your MVP is one core action done well — not five features done poorly.

variants: Make a list of 5 things you WON'T build in the MVP — write them under a header 'NOT YET' in your solution sketch to keep scope from creeping.
Write your 'Not Building List' — 5 features or ideas you're explicitly excluding from version one, so you don't build them by accident.
Create a scope boundary document: list 5 things that sound good but are NOT going in the MVP — every tempting feature you add now delays your first paying user.
tasks.why: Defining what you won't build is as important as defining what you will.

variants: Send your last 2 DMs to hit 17+ total outreach attempts — after tomorrow's review you'll shift from outreach to building mode.
Fire 2 more DMs from your list — tomorrow is your week-2 review so close out the outreach phase strong.
Send 2 final outreach messages from your target list today — day 14 is review day, so this is your last push on validation DMs.
tasks.why: Closing strong on outreach gives you the clearest possible picture for tomorrow's review.

variants: Write a one-page 'Validation Summary': the problem, who has it acutely, what they currently pay or do, the key quote that stuck with you most, and your confidence level (1-10).
Summarize your entire validation phase in one page: target user, problem statement, current solutions, 3 user quotes, and your go/no-go confidence rating from 1-10.
Create a Validation Summary doc with 5 sections: problem, user, current workaround, best user quote, and your confidence score — this becomes your source of truth for building.
tasks.why: A written validation summary forces honest assessment before you spend time building.

variants: Open Framer, Carrd, or Webflow — create a free account if you don't have one — and bookmark 3 landing page templates that could work for your product.
Sign up for Framer or Carrd right now and browse their templates — pick 3 that match your vibe and bookmark them for the landing page you're building next week.
Create an account on whichever landing page tool you'll use (Framer, Carrd, or Webflow) and save 3 templates you could realistically ship in a week.
tasks.why: Landing page tools chosen now means you build next week — not research tools next week.

variants: Open your Validation Log and review every conversation from the past 2 weeks — write the final count: total DMs sent, conversations had, and strong-pain users identified.
Do your full 2-week validation review: tally DMs sent, replies received, calls or voice exchanges completed, and the number of people who said they'd pay for a solution.
Review all 14 days of outreach in your log: count total contacts made, total responses, total 'I'd pay for this' signals — write those 3 numbers as your validation scorecard.
tasks.why: Your validation data determines whether you build — don't skip the honest count.

variants: Write a one-sentence 'Go Decision': either 'I'm building this because [specific evidence]' or 'I'm pivoting because [specific evidence]' — commit to one direction in writing.
Make your official go/no-go call in your Validation Summary: write one sentence with your decision and the single strongest piece of evidence supporting it.
Commit to your direction in writing today: 'Building [product] for [user] because [evidence]' or 'Not building this because [evidence]' — no hedging, pick a lane.
tasks.why: A written decision creates accountability — ambiguity is the enemy of shipping.

variants: Open the landing page template you chose and write your headline: one sentence that names the user, the outcome, and the time frame — spend 10 minutes writing 5 versions and pick the sharpest one.
Draft 5 landing page headlines right now in your chosen tool — each should name who the product is for and what it does for them — then pick the best one and paste it into the template.
Go to your landing page builder, open your chosen template, and write 5 headline variations for your product — then select the one that's clearest and most specific and set it as the hero text.
tasks.why: Your headline is the most read element on the page — it decides whether users scroll or leave.

variants: Open your landing page template and write the subheadline: one sentence that explains how your product delivers the headline's promise — keep it under 15 words.
Write your landing page subheadline now — it should answer 'how does this work?' in one plain sentence under 15 words.
Draft the subheadline for your landing page: one sentence that clarifies the mechanism or approach behind your headline promise.
tasks.why: The subheadline converts scanners to readers — it needs to add specificity, not repetition.

variants: Write 3 bullet points for your landing page describing the 3 core outcomes users get — use numbers or time frames in at least 2 of them.
Add 3 benefit bullets to your landing page: each one describes a specific outcome the user gets, not a feature of your product — make 2 of them include a number.
Write your landing page's value bullets — 3 short statements, each describing what the user achieves, not what the tool does — include at least one time-saving or money-saving metric.
tasks.why: Outcome-focused bullets sell the result — feature bullets sell confusion.

variants: Add an email capture form to your landing page with a simple CTA button — 'Join the Waitlist' or 'Get Early Access' — and connect it to a Mailchimp or ConvertKit free account.
Set up your waitlist form on the landing page: add an email input, write a CTA button label, and connect the form to a free email tool (Mailchimp, ConvertKit, or Beehiiv).
Integrate an email signup form into your landing page right now — pick a free tool to capture the emails (Mailchimp or ConvertKit work), add the CTA, and confirm submissions are going through.
tasks.why: An email form on day 15 means you can start collecting signups the moment you share the link.

variants: Publish your landing page right now — even if it's not perfect, make it live with a real URL you can share.
Hit publish on your landing page today — don't wait for perfect copy or a custom domain, get it live with the default URL.
Make your landing page live right now: publish it, copy the URL, and paste it somewhere you can find it — done is better than perfect.
tasks.why: A live page gets signups — a draft gets nothing.

variants: Share your landing page URL in a relevant subreddit where your target users hang out — post it as a 'Show HN'-style share or as a comment in a relevant thread.
Post your landing page link in a relevant Reddit thread or subreddit today — frame it as 'I built this because of [problem], curious if others feel the same way.'
Drop your landing page link in one Reddit community relevant to your problem — give context about why you built it and ask for honest feedback.
tasks.why: Reddit is your first real traffic test — it tells you if your message resonates with strangers.

variants: Post about your product on IndieHackers today — share your validation story, what problem you're solving, and your landing page link — ask for feedback.
Write an IndieHackers post about your product: describe the problem, your 2-week validation process, and link to your landing page — post it today.
Publish your first IndieHackers update: 3-4 paragraphs on the problem you validated, the users you talked to, and the landing page you just launched — include the link.
tasks.why: IndieHackers is built for this moment — founders share their journey there and get early adopters.

variants: Check your email capture tool and count signups from yesterday's shares — log the number in your notes and note which platform sent the most traffic.
Open your email tool (Mailchimp/ConvertKit) and check your signup count from launch day — write the number down and note where you shared the link.
Log your day-1 waitlist stats: total signups, which platform drove them (Reddit vs. IndieHackers), and the page's bounce behavior if you have analytics.
tasks.why: Launch day metrics tell you which channel works — double down on the one that's sending signups.

variants: Post your landing page on Twitter/X today — write a 3-tweet thread: tweet 1 is the problem, tweet 2 is your solution, tweet 3 is the link with a question asking for feedback.
Share your product on Twitter/X as a thread: first tweet names the problem, second tweet describes what you're building, third tweet links to your landing page and asks a question.
Write a 3-part Twitter/X thread about your product: frame tweet 1 around the user pain, tweet 2 around your approach, tweet 3 as the landing page link with 'Would this help you?'
tasks.why: Twitter/X threads generate engagement and shares — the format forces you to be concise and clear.

variants: DM your top 5 validation contacts from the first 2 weeks and tell them the landing page is live — send them the link and ask if they'd sign up.
Message the 5 most engaged people from your validation outreach — tell them the page is live, send the link, and ask for their honest reaction.
Reach back out to your strongest validation contacts from days 1-14 and say 'the landing page is live — here's the link, would love your reaction.'
tasks.why: Warm contacts convert better than cold traffic — they're already sold on the problem.

variants: Check your running signup total and write today's count in your notes — you need 20 waitlist signups by day 30.
Log your current waitlist number right now — count where you are and write how many more signups you need to hit 20.
Open your email tool and note today's total signup count — track this daily until you hit your 20-signup milestone.
tasks.why: Daily tracking keeps you honest about whether your sharing is actually working.

variants: Find 2 Slack or Discord communities where your target users are active and join them — post an introduction and ask one genuine question related to your problem.
Join 2 new online communities (Slack groups, Discord servers, or Facebook groups) where your target user hangs out — introduce yourself and engage with one thread today.
Look up 2 Slack or Discord communities in your niche and request to join them — once in, say hello and engage with an existing conversation before sharing anything about your product.
tasks.why: Community presence before sharing is the difference between spam and contribution.

variants: Look at your landing page and rewrite any section where you used jargon or generic phrases — replace them with the exact words your users used during validation.
Audit your landing page copy for vague language and replace it with specific phrases pulled directly from your validation conversations.
Do a copy pass on your landing page: for every generic phrase, substitute a phrase you actually heard from a user during your validation DMs or calls.
tasks.why: Copy that sounds like your users converts better than copy that sounds like a product launch.

variants: Share your landing page in one of the Slack or Discord communities you joined yesterday — frame it as 'I'm building this based on conversations I've had, would love your feedback.'
Post your landing page link in one of your new communities — keep it genuine: explain the problem you saw and ask if the solution resonates with them.
Drop your landing page in one Slack or Discord today — context first, link second — and ask a specific question like 'does this headline make sense to you?'
tasks.why: Community shares from a genuine contributor get engagement — spam from a lurker gets ignored.

variants: Write a short email to your current waitlist subscribers — introduce yourself, explain what you're building and why, and ask them one question about their biggest frustration with the problem.
Send your first email to your waitlist: introduce yourself in 3 sentences, describe the product in 2 sentences, and ask subscribers to reply with their #1 pain point related to the problem.
Email your waitlist today — keep it under 150 words: who you are, what you're building, and one question that invites a reply — replies are gold for your product direction.
tasks.why: A waitlist that's never emailed is a list of strangers — engage them early to build real relationships.

variants: Map out your MVP: draw or list the minimum screens or steps a user would go through to complete the core action — no more than 3-5 steps, no more than 2 screens.
Sketch your MVP flow on paper or in a doc: what does the user do first, second, third? Keep it to 3 steps max and describe each in one sentence.
Create a simple user flow for your MVP: list each step from signup to 'problem solved' — if it's more than 5 steps, cut until it's 3.
tasks.why: A mapped flow prevents scope creep — if you can't draw the MVP in under 5 steps, it's too big.

variants: Log today's waitlist count and calculate how many signups you're averaging per day since launch.
Check your waitlist total and divide by the number of days since launch — write your daily average signup rate.
Open your email tool, note today's subscriber count, and calculate your daily signup rate — you need to hit 20 by day 30.
tasks.why: Knowing your rate tells you whether to push harder on sharing or if the current pace gets you there.

variants: Go to your second community from day 18 and contribute to one existing thread — reply helpfully to a question related to your problem space, no product mention.
Add genuine value to a discussion in your second community today — answer someone's question or share a useful insight, nothing about your product.
Be useful in one of your communities today: find a thread where you can contribute expertise related to your problem space and reply with a specific, helpful answer.
tasks.why: Contribution builds trust — trust makes your eventual product share land differently.

variants: Open your MVP flow from yesterday and write the tech stack you'll use for each step: auth, database, and UI — choose based on what you know, not what sounds impressive.
Decide your tech stack for the MVP today: write down what you'll use for the frontend, backend, and database — keep it to tools you've used before or can learn in a day.
Pick your MVP stack right now: list the specific tools for UI, data storage, auth, and payments — no evaluation paralysis, commit to one option per layer in your notes.
tasks.why: Tech stack decisions made now mean you start building on day 31 without a detour.

variants: Review this week's progress in your notes: how many new waitlist signups did you get, which platform drove the most, and what copy change had the biggest impact?
Do your week-3 review: log waitlist total, identify which share (Reddit, IndieHackers, or Twitter) generated the most signups, and note one thing you'd change about your landing page.
Weekly review: open your notes and check 3 things — current waitlist count, most effective sharing channel, and one landing page element you're going to test or change.
tasks.why: Week-3 review tells you where to concentrate sharing effort in week 4.

variants: Re-share your landing page on the platform that drove the most signups — with a new angle or different context than your original post.
Go back to your best-performing share channel and post again with a different hook — use a user story or a specific stat from your validation instead of repeating your original post.
Post a fresh angle on your top-performing platform today: instead of announcing the product, share a user pain story that naturally leads to your landing page link.
tasks.why: Doubling down on what works beats experimenting with new channels while signups are low.

variants: Email your waitlist subscribers again — share a brief update on what you've built so far and ask for replies from anyone willing to be a beta tester.
Send update email #2 to your waitlist: tell them what's been built, what's coming next, and ask who wants early beta access — get 3 hands raised.
Write a waitlist update email: 2-3 sentences on your progress, 1 sentence asking who wants to be first in when the beta launches — send it today.
tasks.why: Keeping your waitlist warm with progress updates prevents it from going cold by launch day.

variants: Check your waitlist count and note how many people responded to your beta tester ask from yesterday's email.
Log today's waitlist total and count how many people raised their hand for beta access after yesterday's email.
Open your email tool and check who replied to your beta tester request — add their names to a 'Beta List' in your notes.
tasks.why: Beta testers are your most engaged users — knowing who they are before launch is essential.

variants: Connect a Stripe account to your product domain — create a free Stripe account if you don't have one and set your business name and payout account.
Set up Stripe right now: create an account, enter your business info, and connect your bank account so you can accept payments the moment your MVP is live.
Go to stripe.com, create your account, fill in your business details, and get through the onboarding — you want payments ready before you need them.
tasks.why: Having Stripe ready means you can charge your first customer the same day the MVP ships.

variants: Write the copy for your pre-sale email — 'The MVP is live, first 10 customers get lifetime access for $X' — keep it under 200 words and save it as a draft.
Draft your launch email to the waitlist now: announce the MVP is coming, name the price, describe the lifetime deal for the first 10 buyers — save it as a draft to send on day 45.
Write and save your pre-sale email draft today: the subject line, the one-paragraph pitch, the price point for first 10 users, and the Stripe payment link placeholder.
tasks.why: Writing the launch email now forces you to commit to a price before you start building.

variants: Post your landing page in the third community you identified — a different platform than Reddit or IndieHackers, such as a Facebook Group, Discord server, or X community.
Share your landing page in a third community today — if you've hit Reddit and IndieHackers, try Twitter/X, a Facebook Group, or a niche Discord server.
Hit your third sharing channel today: pick a community you haven't shared in yet and post your landing page with a fresh intro and specific question.
tasks.why: Three channels is your minimum — each audience is different and sends different types of users.

variants: Set up your development environment for the MVP — create the project folder, initialize the repo on GitHub, and commit the boilerplate so you're ready to build on day 31.
Initialize your MVP codebase today: create the repo on GitHub, set up the project structure with your chosen stack, and push the first commit — even if it's just a README.
Create your project on GitHub, set up the local dev environment, and run a 'hello world' in your chosen framework — commit and push so day 31 starts with a live foundation.
tasks.why: A working dev environment on day 23 means you code on day 31, not configure.

variants: Check your total waitlist count, calculate the gap to 20, and write 3 specific actions you'll take this week to close that gap before day 30.
Audit your waitlist growth: total current, goal of 20, and a written plan for 3 actions this week to close the gap — post in a new community, send a DM campaign, or try a different post format.
Write a 20-signup plan: current count, number needed, and exactly 3 tactics you'll execute in the next 7 days to hit the milestone — schedule each one in your notes.
tasks.why: A written plan with specific tactics is the only thing that converts a goal into a result.

variants: Execute the first tactic from your day-23 plan — if it was a community post, write and post it now.
Run tactic #1 from your 20-signup plan right now — don't plan further, just execute.
Open your day-23 plan and do the first action on the list — take it from plan to done today.
tasks.why: Plans are worthless without same-day execution — start on tactic 1 today.

variants: Go back to your GitHub repo and write the data model for your MVP — what tables or collections do you need, and what are the 5 most important fields in each?
Define your MVP data model today: list each entity your product needs, the key fields for each, and how they relate — write it in a doc or as comments in your code.
Sketch your database schema for the MVP: what data needs to be stored, how is it structured, and what's the primary key for each table — write it in your dev notes.
tasks.why: A defined data model means you build with a clear target instead of redesigning the database mid-build.

variants: Record a 3-minute Loom video explaining your product — who it's for, what problem it solves, and what the landing page link is — share the Loom link in your next post.
Make a short Loom demo today: 3 minutes, on camera or screen, explaining the problem and what you're building — share the link in a community or DM where it fits.
Create a 3-minute Loom walkthrough of your landing page and product vision — post the link in at least one community or as a follow-up to your existing shares.
tasks.why: A face-on-camera Loom builds trust faster than text alone — conversion rates for video outreach are higher.

variants: Log today's waitlist total — write both the number and how many days remain until your day-30 milestone check.
Check your signup count and calculate: do you hit 20 at your current rate before day 30, or do you need to accelerate?
Open your email tool, note today's subscriber count, and write the daily rate you need for the next 5 days to hit 20 total.
tasks.why: Tracking with a deadline in mind creates urgency that casual monitoring doesn't.

variants: Execute tactic #2 from your 20-signup plan — post in a new community, send a targeted DM campaign, or try a new post format.
Run the second tactic from your day-23 plan today — no adjustments unless tactic 1 completely failed, just execute.
Move to tactic #2 from your growth plan: do it today, not tomorrow — consistency in execution is what separates those who hit milestones from those who miss them.
tasks.why: Two tactics in two days keeps momentum — spreading them out allows procrastination.

variants: Build the first screen of your MVP in code — even if it's just the home/landing view in your framework, get something rendering in your local dev environment.
Start coding your MVP today: open your IDE, build the first route or screen, and get something displaying on localhost — it doesn't need to look good yet.
Write the first real code for your MVP now: scaffold your first screen, get it rendering locally, and commit the progress to GitHub before you close your laptop.
tasks.why: Shipping day 31 starts with writing code on day 25 — you can't compress 6 days into 1.

variants: Check your waitlist total and compare it to where you expected to be by now — write one sentence on the gap or surplus.
Log today's signup count and note whether you're ahead or behind your pace to 20 signups by day 30.
Open your email tool, note the subscriber count, and mark whether you're on track, ahead, or behind your day-30 goal.
tasks.why: Daily awareness of the gap creates the right kind of urgency.

variants: Build one more feature or screen in your MVP codebase — focus on the core user action you defined on day 12, not polish or navigation.
Add the next critical piece of your MVP in code today: the input, button, or logic that handles the core user action — commit your progress.
Write the code for the most important user interaction in your MVP — the one where the user actually does the thing that solves the problem — commit to GitHub when done.
tasks.why: One meaningful coding session per day for 30 days is how MVPs get built.

variants: Write a Stripe product in your dashboard: set a name, price, and one-time payment or subscription — this is the 'first 10 lifetime deal' price you'll offer at launch.
Go into Stripe and create your first product and price: name it, set the dollar amount for the lifetime deal, and save the payment link URL.
Set up your launch offer in Stripe right now: create a product called '[Product Name] Early Access', set the price, and copy the payment link into your pre-sale email draft.
tasks.why: A live Stripe payment link means your launch email can go out the same day the MVP does.

variants: Execute tactic #3 from your 20-signup plan — this is your last planned sharing push before the day-30 milestone.
Run your third growth tactic from your day-23 plan today — it's your last scheduled push before the milestone check.
Deploy tactic 3 from your day-23 plan right now: post, DM, or share — get it done before the end of today.
tasks.why: The third tactic closes out your planned signup push — see what the 3 combined tactics produced.

variants: Build the data persistence layer of your MVP: connect your chosen database (Supabase is recommended) and write the basic read/write operations for your core data model.
Connect your MVP to a database today: set up Supabase or your chosen DB, create the tables from your day-24 schema, and confirm a basic save/fetch works.
Wire up the database for your MVP: create your tables in Supabase, write the save function for your core entity, and test that data persists after a page reload.
tasks.why: Data persistence is the line between a demo and an actual product — cross it today.

variants: DM 3 of your strongest validation contacts and ask them if they'd be willing to try a very rough beta version next week in exchange for the lifetime deal price.
Reach out to your 3 best early users from validation and offer them first access to the beta — in exchange for honest feedback within 48 hours of using it.
Message the 3 most engaged people from your validation phase and ask if they want to be your first beta testers next week — be specific: 'rough version, free to use, I need your feedback.'
tasks.why: Pre-committed beta testers mean someone is waiting on you to ship — that's the best deadline there is.

variants: Do your week-4 review: check current waitlist count, review the last 7 days of growth, and note which of your 3 tactics from the plan actually moved the needle.
Week-4 review in your notes: final tally on tactics 1-3 from your growth plan, current waitlist total, and whether you're on track to hit 20 by day 30.
Open your tracker and do a week-4 audit: how many signups per tactic, total current count, and an honest assessment of which sharing approach worked best.
tasks.why: Week reviews tell you what to repeat and what to drop — essential data for the next phase.

variants: Build or wire up authentication in your MVP — use Supabase Auth or your chosen auth provider and confirm a test user can sign up and log in.
Add user authentication to your MVP today: implement sign-up and log-in flows using Supabase Auth or similar, and test that it works end to end.
Set up auth for your MVP: use Supabase Auth (or NextAuth if you're on Next.js), create a test account, log in, and confirm the session persists on refresh.
tasks.why: Auth is the gate to everything — without it, you can't personalize or protect any user data.

variants: Write your week-4 IndieHackers update: share your current signup count, what validation taught you, what you're building, and when beta will be live — post it today.
Publish a progress update on IndieHackers: current waitlist number, what you learned from validation, your MVP tech stack, and your target ship date — be specific.
Post a week-4 build journal entry on IndieHackers with these exact details: waitlist count, validation lessons, current build status, and your beta launch date.
tasks.why: Public progress posts attract users and hold you accountable to your own timeline.

variants: Check your waitlist total one day before your milestone — write the number and whether you hit 20 or not.
Log today's subscriber count: are you at 20 yet? If not, how many short are you and is there one quick action to get there today?
Open your email tool and check your total — if you're at 20, great. If not, write what you'll do in the next 24 hours to close the gap.
tasks.why: The day before a milestone is the last chance to make a push before the checkpoint.

variants: If you haven't hit 20 signups yet, DM 5 people from your network personally and ask them to sign up — not a mass message, a personal note to each one.
Short of 20 signups? Send 5 personal DMs to people who know you and would support the launch — ask directly: 'Would you sign up for my waitlist?'
If you're not at 20 signups, reach out to 5 people individually right now — friends, colleagues, or network contacts — and ask for their support.
tasks.why: Personal asks convert at a higher rate than any public post — use them when you need a push.

variants: Build the most critical remaining feature of your MVP that isn't done yet — commit the code and note what percentage of the core flow is complete.
Code the next biggest gap in your MVP today — look at your user flow from day 19 and build whichever step is still missing from your local version.
Open your MVP flow diagram from day 19, identify the biggest incomplete piece, and build it today — commit the code and update your build progress notes.
tasks.why: Shipping day 31 requires every critical step in your flow to be coded before then.

variants: Count your final waitlist total for the month — write it down, celebrate if you hit 20, and note exactly what drove the most signups over the 30 days.
Do your 30-day milestone check: final waitlist count, top traffic source, and one sentence on what you'd do differently in this validation phase if you started over.
Mark your 30-day checkpoint: log your final signup count, your best channel, and your honest assessment of the problem/market fit you've found — write it in your Validation Summary.
tasks.why: Day 30 is the end of phase 1 — an honest checkpoint sets the right foundation for building.

variants: Review the goal you set on day 1 and write a one-paragraph update: what changed about the problem, the user, or the solution since you started, and what's sharper now?
Compare your day-1 problem statement to where you stand today: what's the same, what changed, and what's the most valuable thing validation taught you?
Write a 'Phase 1 Lessons' entry in your notes: 3 things you learned about the problem or user that you didn't know on day 1, and how each one changed your product direction.
tasks.why: Explicit lesson documentation prevents repeating mistakes in the build phase.

variants: Write your Phase 2 goal for days 31-60: ship MVP in 2 weeks, get 5 paying users — write the specific date you're targeting for the MVP launch and the price you'll charge.
Set your Phase 2 targets in writing: MVP live by [specific date], 5 paying customers by day 60, lifetime deal price of $[X] — commit these numbers in your notes.
Define the next 30 days in one doc: MVP ship date, target price for the lifetime deal, the 5 users you'll email first, and your single success metric for phase 2.
tasks.why: Written targets with specific dates create accountability — vague intentions don't.

variants: Open your MVP codebase, run it locally, and click through every step in your user flow from signup to core action — note every broken or missing step.
Boot your local dev environment right now and walk through your MVP end to end as if you were a first-time user — write down every step that fails or is missing.
Run your MVP locally and test the complete user journey from day 19's flow diagram — list every gap between where the product is and where it needs to be.
tasks.why: You can't ship what you haven't tested — today's audit defines the exact remaining build list.

variants: Write a 'Ship List' of the remaining tasks to get your MVP live — every item must be completable in under 2 hours, and the full list must fit inside 2 weeks.
Create a build sprint list: every remaining coding task to make the MVP functional, each estimated in hours — if the total exceeds 80 hours, cut features until it doesn't.
List every remaining development task for your MVP and assign each a time estimate — if the total exceeds 2 weeks of work, remove features until it fits.
tasks.why: A realistic ship list with estimates is the difference between a deadline and a wish.

variants: Code the first item on your ship list — spend 8 focused minutes on it right now and commit the result to GitHub.
Pick the highest-priority item from your ship list and spend 8 minutes writing the code — commit whatever progress you make.
Execute item #1 from your ship list: open the code, write it, commit it — 8 minutes of focused building beats 2 hours of planning.
tasks.why: Phase 2 starts today — the clock is running toward your first paying customer.

variants: Open your ship list and check off anything completed — then pick today's item and commit to finishing it before you close your laptop.
Review your day-31 ship list: mark done, mark in-progress, and identify the single most critical item to complete today.
Look at your build sprint list, update the status of each item, and write the one task you will complete entirely today.
tasks.why: Daily review of the ship list keeps your build honest and scope-tight.

variants: Build today's MVP task: set a 5-minute timer and write code without stopping — commit progress even if it's not finished.
Write code for 5 focused minutes on today's ship list item — no Slack, no Reddit, no switching — then commit whatever you have.
Execute today's build item: 5 minutes, one task, one commit — ship something every single day of this sprint.
tasks.why: Daily shipping habit beats marathon sessions — consistent output compounds.

variants: Deploy your current MVP state to a live URL using Vercel — even if it's broken in places, get it on a real URL so your beta testers can access it.
Push your MVP to Vercel right now: connect your GitHub repo, deploy, and share the live URL with yourself — broken features are fine, a live URL is essential.
Get your MVP live on Vercel today: import your repo, click deploy, and confirm the URL is accessible — beta testers can't use a localhost.
tasks.why: A live URL is the difference between a project and a product — deploy it now.

variants: Send your live Vercel URL to your 3 pre-committed beta testers from day 27 — tell them it's rough and ask for one piece of feedback within 24 hours.
Message your beta testers the live URL right now — say 'it's live, it's rough, what's the first thing that confuses you?' — expect replies within a day.
Share your MVP URL with the beta testers you recruited on day 27 — tell them the build is in progress and ask them to try the core action and report what breaks.
tasks.why: Beta feedback today shapes the last week of building — waiting loses you that feedback loop.

variants: Write and execute the next 2 items on your ship list — pick the ones that are closest to done and finish them.
Complete 2 more ship list items today: choose the two smallest remaining tasks and close them out with commits.
Pick 2 remaining items from your ship list, code them, commit them — preferably the two that unblock the most other tasks.
tasks.why: Two items done per day means your ship list empties in the time you have.

variants: Log your build progress in your notes: items completed, items remaining, and your honest assessment of whether you'll hit your MVP launch date.
Update your ship list with current status and calculate: given what's done and what's left, will you hit your phase-2 launch date from day 30?
Write a build status update: completed tasks, remaining tasks, time estimate for the remainder, and a yes/no on whether you'll hit your MVP ship date.
tasks.why: Honest progress tracking prevents the painful surprise of realizing you're 2 weeks behind.

variants: Check your messages for beta tester feedback — log every piece of feedback in a 'Beta Feedback' doc, no matter how small.
Open your messages, read all beta tester responses, and add every piece of feedback — positive or negative — to your Beta Feedback tracker.
Review any feedback your beta testers sent and write each piece of it verbatim in a new 'Beta Feedback' document, with the tester's name next to each item.
tasks.why: Collected feedback you can't find is the same as feedback you never got.

variants: Fix the top bug or UX issue reported by your beta testers — the one that makes the core action impossible or confusing — and redeploy.
Address the single most critical piece of beta feedback right now: open the code, fix it, redeploy to Vercel, and notify your testers.
Implement the most urgent beta fix today: identify it from your feedback log, write the code, push to GitHub, and confirm the Vercel deployment updated.
tasks.why: Fast beta iteration builds trust with testers and improves your product faster than solo building.

variants: Send your 3 beta testers a follow-up message asking specifically: 'Did you complete [core action]? What stopped you or slowed you down?'
Message each beta tester with a targeted question: 'Were you able to [do the core thing]? If not, where did you get stuck?'
Follow up with each beta tester today: ask them if they completed the main user action and what the biggest friction point was.
tasks.why: Knowing where users get stuck is more valuable than knowing what they liked.

variants: Do your week-5 review: list every ship list item completed this week, tally the remaining items, and note whether your launch date is still realistic.
Review the past 7 days of building: count completed ship list items, remaining items, and write your honest go/no-go on hitting your planned launch date.
Week-5 checkpoint: open your ship list, count done vs. remaining, update your time estimates, and write one sentence on what's most at risk of slipping.
tasks.why: Week reviews in build phase prevent slow drift into a launch date that never comes.

variants: Build the next 2 items on your ship list — prioritize anything that's blocking a complete end-to-end user flow.
Complete 2 more ship list items today — focus on anything that prevents a user from getting from sign-up to core action without your help.
Ship 2 more list items: pick the ones that, when done, let a new user complete the full product flow without getting stuck.
tasks.why: Unblocking the full user flow is the most valuable build work at this stage.

variants: Post a week-5 build update on IndieHackers or Twitter/X — share your MVP URL, what works, what's still rough, and invite people to try it.
Share your live MVP link publicly today with an honest update: 'This is rough but functional — here's what it does, here's what's broken, feedback welcome.'
Write a public build journal post this week: share the live URL, describe the core feature that works, be honest about what's still broken, and ask for testers.
tasks.why: Public shipping attracts early users and creates momentum that solo building doesn't.

variants: Read your Beta Feedback doc from start to finish and underline the 3 pieces of feedback that came up more than once.
Review all beta feedback collected so far and mark any issue or suggestion that more than one tester mentioned.
Open your Beta Feedback tracker and identify the pattern: what did multiple testers struggle with or mention independently?
tasks.why: Repeated feedback from multiple users is a signal — one person's complaint can be an edge case, two is a pattern.

variants: Fix the second most critical beta issue from your feedback log — code it, commit, redeploy, notify testers.
Address the second bug or UX problem on your Beta Feedback list: make the code change, push to Vercel, and message your testers that it's fixed.
Close out your second beta fix today: write the code, commit, deploy — send your testers a message that the update is live.
tasks.why: Rapid iteration on beta feedback shows testers you're responsive — that trust converts to paying customers.

variants: Complete 2 more ship list items and update your build log with current progress and revised launch date estimate.
Knock out 2 more tasks from your ship list and update your sprint doc with the current item count — done, in-progress, remaining.
Ship 2 more items from your list today and log the update: total completed, total remaining, new estimate for launch-ready date.
tasks.why: Daily progress + daily tracking compounds into a launch-ready product.

variants: Ask your 3 beta testers to try the updated version — send each one a direct message with the specific thing you fixed and ask if it's better.
Notify your beta testers of the latest fixes — message each one and ask them to re-test the specific flow that was broken.
Ping your beta testers with the fix update: 'I addressed [issue] — can you try [action] again and let me know if it's better?'
tasks.why: Closing the feedback loop signals professionalism and keeps testers engaged.

variants: Add a custom domain to your Vercel deployment — buy one from Namecheap or use one you own, and configure the DNS settings today.
Set up a real domain for your MVP: purchase one (Namecheap is cheap and fast), add it to your Vercel project, and confirm it resolves.
Get a domain for your product today — pick something short and memorable, buy it, connect it to Vercel, and update your landing page link accordingly.
tasks.why: A custom domain signals legitimacy — users trust products at real URLs more than Vercel subdomains.

variants: Complete 2 more items from your ship list — you should be more than halfway through the original list by now.
Ship 2 more tasks from your sprint list today — check your progress and confirm you're past the halfway point of the original build plan.
Build and commit 2 more ship list items — calculate the percentage complete and write it in your build log.
tasks.why: Hitting the halfway point of your ship list on day 37 keeps you on pace for an on-time launch.

variants: Verify your custom domain is resolving correctly by visiting it in a browser — if it's not working, troubleshoot the DNS settings now.
Test your custom domain: open it in an incognito window and confirm the MVP loads correctly — fix any DNS or redirect issues.
Check that your domain is live: visit it in a browser, confirm it loads your MVP, and confirm the SSL certificate is valid.
tasks.why: A broken domain on launch day kills conversions — check it now while you still have time to fix it.

variants: Add Stripe payment integration to your MVP — at minimum, add a payment link that a user can click to pay for access.
Wire up Stripe to your MVP today: add a button that opens your Stripe payment link — that's the minimum viable payment flow.
Integrate payments into your MVP: add a 'Get Access' button that links to your Stripe payment page — test it with a test mode transaction.
tasks.why: Payment integration is the last gate to a paying customer — it must work before launch.

variants: Test the full payment flow end to end: click your payment button, use a Stripe test card, complete the checkout, and confirm the success state triggers correctly.
Run a complete Stripe test: go through the payment flow using Stripe's test card number, confirm the webhook or redirect fires, and log the result.
Do a payment flow QA pass: simulate a full purchase using Stripe test mode, verify the confirmation state appears, and fix anything that doesn't work.
tasks.why: A broken checkout on launch day is the most expensive bug you can have.

variants: Complete 2 more ship list items — focus on any items that touch the post-payment user experience so customers get value immediately after paying.
Build 2 more ship list items today — prioritize anything that affects what a user sees or can do right after completing payment.
Ship 2 more tasks from your list: ideally ones that shape the post-purchase experience — what does a paying customer do first?
tasks.why: First impressions after payment determine whether a user becomes an advocate or a refund request.

variants: Invite 2 more people to beta test — find them from your waitlist or your validation contacts from days 1-14 and send a personal DM.
Expand your beta pool by 2 testers: message 2 people from your waitlist who seem engaged and offer them free access in exchange for feedback.
Recruit 2 more beta testers from your waitlist today — pick the most active subscribers and offer early access in exchange for a 10-minute feedback session.
tasks.why: More testers means faster bug discovery — 5 testers find more than 3 ever will.

variants: Write the onboarding sequence for new users: what 3 things should a new user do in their first 5 minutes to get value from the product?
Design your new-user onboarding: write a 3-step sequence (tooltip, empty state message, or welcome email) that guides the user to their first success moment.
Draft your onboarding flow: what happens when a brand-new user logs in for the first time? Write 3 steps that lead them to the core action within 5 minutes.
tasks.why: Users who don't get value in the first session don't come back — onboarding prevents that.

variants: Review your ship list and count how many items remain — write the number and your revised launch date if the timeline has shifted.
Open your build sprint list, mark current status on every item, and confirm whether your launch date is still accurate or needs to move.
Update your ship list: cross off everything completed, count what's left, and write your honest launch-ready date at the top of the doc.
tasks.why: A weekly ship list review keeps your launch date honest — denial is the most common builder mistake.

variants: Implement your onboarding flow from yesterday — at minimum, add a welcome message or empty state that tells new users exactly what to do first.
Build step 1 of your onboarding sequence: add a welcome prompt, tooltip, or empty-state message that directs a new user to take the core action.
Code your onboarding first step today: whatever appears when a new user logs in should tell them exactly what to do — implement it and test it with a fresh test account.
tasks.why: Onboarding built before launch means testers experience the product as customers will.

variants: Send a check-in message to all 5 beta testers asking them one specific question: 'What's the one thing that would make you pay $[X] for this right now?'
Message every beta tester today with a direct question: 'If I charged $[your price] for this, what would need to be better for you to say yes?'
Ask your beta panel a pricing question today: 'At $[X]/month or $[Y] lifetime, what's missing that would make this a no-brainer purchase for you?'
tasks.why: Willingness-to-pay questions reveal your biggest product gap before you launch.

variants: Review beta tester responses to your pricing question from yesterday and add the top 3 answers to your Beta Feedback doc with a note on what each means for your build.
Read every reply to yesterday's pricing question and log the top insights — focus on what product change would flip each 'not yet' into a 'yes'.
Compile the pricing feedback from your beta testers: what did they say was missing? Add the top 2-3 answers to your feedback doc and flag which ones are fast to fix.
tasks.why: Pre-launch willingness-to-pay signals are the most actionable data you can collect.

variants: Fix or build the highest-impact item from your beta pricing feedback — the change most likely to convert a 'maybe' into a paying customer.
Implement the top change from your pricing feedback today: code it, deploy, and message the tester who flagged it that you've addressed it.
Build the single thing your testers said was missing before they'd pay: prioritize it above everything else on your ship list and close it today.
tasks.why: Building for willingness-to-pay is the most direct path to your first sale.

variants: Complete 2 more ship list items and commit — write in your build log the total remaining items and your launch date confidence level from 1 to 10.
Ship 2 more tasks from your sprint list and update your build status — rate your confidence in the launch date from 1-10 and explain the rating.
Knock out 2 more ship list items, redeploy, and do a confidence check: are you going to hit your launch date? Write your honest rating and why.
tasks.why: Explicit confidence tracking forces honesty before the launch date becomes a crisis.

variants: Do your week-6 review: how many ship list items remain, what's your revised launch date, and what's the single biggest risk to shipping on time?
Week-6 checkpoint: open your build doc, count remaining tasks, confirm or update your launch date, and write the top 1 thing that could derail your timeline.
Review week 6: tasks completed, tasks remaining, launch date status, and your top blocker — write all 4 answers in your build log.
tasks.why: Naming your top risk explicitly is the first step to mitigating it.

variants: Build 2 more ship list items — if you're close to the end of the list, spend this time on polish: fixing errors, improving empty states, or cleaning up copy.
Complete 2 more tasks from your ship list — if the list is nearly done, use this session for QA: test the full user flow from sign-up to payment on a clean account.
Ship 2 more list items or do a full product walkthrough if the list is nearly complete — record any bugs you find in a 'QA Issues' doc.
tasks.why: The last 20% of building is polish and QA — don't skip it in the sprint to launch.

variants: Update your pre-sale email draft from day 22 with your current product description, live URL, and Stripe payment link — make sure the copy reflects what you've actually built.
Revise your launch email draft: replace placeholder copy with the real product description, insert your live domain, and update the Stripe link — save the updated version.
Open your day-22 pre-sale email draft and edit it to reflect the actual product you built: update the description, add the live URL, embed the Stripe payment link.
tasks.why: Your launch email must describe the product you actually built — not the one you imagined on day 22.

variants: Do a full QA pass of your MVP: click through every screen, try every action, and log every bug or broken thing in your QA Issues doc.
Walk through your entire product as a first-time user on a fresh browser profile and write down everything that's confusing, broken, or missing.
Open an incognito window, sign up for your own product as a new user, and note every friction point you hit from landing page to core action.
tasks.why: You can't find bugs you don't look for — a structured QA pass before launch catches the embarrassing ones.

variants: Fix the top 2 bugs from your QA issues list — the ones that would most embarrass you if a paying customer hit them.
Address the 2 most critical QA issues you found: code the fixes, deploy, and re-test both in a fresh browser window.
Close out the top 2 items from your QA Issues doc: write the fix, push to Vercel, and confirm they're resolved by testing them again.
tasks.why: Known bugs that you ship anyway become your first support tickets — fix them now.

variants: Write a 'Known Issues' doc with every remaining bug you won't fix before launch — this is your post-launch backlog, not an excuse to ignore them.
Create a post-launch bug backlog: list every QA issue you won't fix before launch, ranked by severity — plan to address the top 3 within the first week after launch.
Document your remaining QA issues in a backlog: what's known, what's priority, and what's acceptable to ship with — write your plan to close each one within 2 weeks of launch.
tasks.why: A documented backlog means you're choosing what to ship, not ignoring what's broken.

variants: Write your launch announcement tweet — one tweet that names the problem, names who it's for, and links to your product — save it as a draft in Twitter/X.
Draft your launch tweet right now: describe the problem in sentence 1, your solution in sentence 2, and the URL in sentence 3 — save it as a draft.
Write your Twitter/X launch post: the problem, the product, the link — keep it under 280 characters and save it as a scheduled or draft tweet.
tasks.why: Writing launch copy in advance means you publish on launch day instead of stressing over words.

variants: Write your launch Reddit post — title that hooks, 2-3 paragraphs on the problem and solution, and your product link — save it as a draft.
Draft your launch post for Reddit: a non-spammy title, a brief story about the problem you experienced, and your product as the solution — save the draft.
Write the Reddit launch post you'll publish on launch day: the problem story, your solution, and the link — save it and don't publish until you're ready to launch.
tasks.why: Pre-written launch posts mean you execute on launch day instead of write.

variants: Fix the remaining top-priority items from your QA backlog — close as many as you can in 8 minutes, commit and deploy after each one.
Work through your QA backlog for 8 minutes: fix, commit, deploy — prioritize by impact on first-time user experience.
Close out QA items from your backlog: spend 8 minutes fixing the highest-severity issues and committing each fix — redeploy when done.
tasks.why: Every QA fix you close before launch is one fewer support ticket after.

variants: Send your pre-sale launch email to your entire waitlist right now — use the updated draft from day 42, include the Stripe link, and send it.
Hit send on your launch email: open your email tool, load your day-42 draft, check the Stripe link one more time, and send it to your full waitlist.
Launch your pre-sale: open your email platform, find your launch email draft, confirm the payment link works, and send it to every subscriber on your list.
tasks.why: The pre-sale email is your first commercial moment — send it while your MVP momentum is live.

variants: Post your launch tweet and Reddit post — both drafts are ready from yesterday, publish them right now within the same hour as your launch email.
Go live on Twitter/X and Reddit today: publish your launch tweet and Reddit post within minutes of each other to maximize the launch window.
Publish your launch posts: post your Twitter/X launch tweet and your Reddit post — do both within the same hour as the email so everything hits simultaneously.
tasks.why: Coordinated multi-channel launch creates a bigger wave than spreading posts out.

variants: DM your 5 best beta testers and 5 validation contacts personally today — tell them the launch is live and ask them to forward your email to one person who would benefit.
Message your top 10 contacts — beta testers and validation people — directly today: 'The launch is live, would you forward this to one person who needs this?'
Send 10 personal DMs on launch day: your beta testers and strongest validation contacts — ask them to share the launch with one specific person they know.
tasks.why: Personal asks from real contacts drive the first sales — algorithms don't convert, people do.

variants: Check your Stripe dashboard right now and log your payment count — how many of the first 10 lifetime deal slots are taken?
Open Stripe and count completed payments from your launch — write the number and note how many lifetime deal slots remain.
Log your launch-day-plus-one sales: check Stripe, count payments, write the total and the gap to your goal of 5 paying users.
tasks.why: Tracking sales daily from launch creates the feedback loop that drives your next move.

variants: Reply to every email response to your launch email — thank them, answer questions, and if they're hesitant, ask one question about what's holding them back.
Respond to every launch email reply today: thank engaged responders, address objections from hesitant ones, and ask a follow-up question to anyone who didn't convert.
Go through every reply to your launch email and respond to each one personally — make each reply specific to what they said, not a copy-paste template.
tasks.why: Personal responses to launch replies convert more than any retargeting ad.

variants: Post a follow-up launch update on IndieHackers: share the launch day results, how many emails sent, how many clicks, how many sales — be transparent with the numbers.
Publish a launch day recap on IndieHackers: total waitlist size, email open rate, clicks, and sales so far — be honest even if the numbers are small.
Write a launch update post on IndieHackers with your real numbers: emails sent, opens, clicks to Stripe, and purchases — share the story, not just the outcome.
tasks.why: Transparent launch posts on IndieHackers attract your next users and build founder credibility.

variants: Check Stripe again — log today's payment count and the delta since yesterday — note the 24-hour sales velocity.
Open Stripe, count total payments to date, calculate the change since yesterday, and write both numbers in your notes.
Log day-2 post-launch sales: Stripe payment count, change from yesterday, and whether you've hit 5 paying users yet.
tasks.why: Daily Stripe checks in launch week tell you if you need to do more outreach or if word is spreading.

variants: DM every person who clicked your Stripe link but didn't complete payment — you can find this in Stripe's checkout sessions — and ask what stopped them.
Look at Stripe's abandoned checkouts and message anyone who started the payment flow but didn't finish — ask one question: what stopped you?
Check your Stripe checkout sessions for incomplete payments and reach out to those users directly: 'Noticed you started the checkout — is there anything I can answer?'
tasks.why: Abandoned checkouts are warm leads — they almost bought, which means a single question can convert them.

variants: Set up a welcome email for new paying customers: a short email that thanks them, links to the product, and gives them one tip for getting started — automate it in your email tool.
Create an automated welcome email for paying customers: thank them, give them the product URL, and tell them one thing to do first — trigger it on Stripe payment success.
Write and automate a 'You're in' email for new customers: congratulations, here's the link, here's your first step — set it to send automatically when Stripe registers a payment.
tasks.why: A fast, warm welcome email reduces churn before the customer even uses the product.

variants: Call or message your first paying customer and thank them personally — ask one question about what made them decide to buy.
Message your first paying customer directly: 'Thank you for buying — what made you pull the trigger?' — their answer is your best sales copy.
Reach out personally to your first customer: thank them, ask what convinced them to pay, and note their exact words in your Beta Feedback doc.
tasks.why: Knowing why your first customer bought tells you what to lead with in every future sales message.

variants: Onboard your first paying customer personally: send them a message offering a 10-minute walkthrough and help them complete the core action in the product.
Offer your first customer a personal onboarding session — a 10-minute call or Loom walkthrough — make sure they get value from the product today.
DM your first paying customer and offer to walk them through the product personally — 10 minutes, on a call or via Loom — make their first experience exceptional.
tasks.why: Manual onboarding for your first customers creates raving fans — don't skip it to scale.

variants: Fix the next 2 items from your QA backlog — your first paying customers deserve a product that works, so close these before more people buy.
Work through 2 more QA backlog items today: your paying customers are using the product now, so prioritize fixes that affect their daily experience.
Close 2 more post-launch bug fixes from your backlog — rank by impact on paying customers' core workflow and fix the top two.
tasks.why: Post-launch bug velocity signals to customers whether you're maintaining or abandoning the product.
