variants: Do your week-7 review: total paying customers, total waitlist, open rate on your launch email, and your single biggest insight from the first week post-launch.
Week-7 checkpoint: log paying users, total sales, email engagement stats, and one lesson from the launch week you'd apply to the next campaign.
Review week 7: current Stripe total, email open rate, top objection you heard, and what you'd do differently on your next launch email — write all four.
tasks.why: First launch week data is the most concentrated feedback you'll get — review it explicitly.

variants: Send a follow-up launch email to non-buyers on your waitlist — this one focuses on a specific problem and ends with 'here's what the product does about it' plus the Stripe link.
Write and send a second sales email to waitlist subscribers who didn't buy yet — use a specific user story or pain scenario as the hook instead of a product announcement.
Send email #2 to non-purchasers: open with a specific story about the problem, close with the product link and price — make this one more personal than the first.
tasks.why: Most buyers need 2-3 touches before purchasing — a follow-up email doubles your conversion window.

variants: Schedule a 10-minute feedback call with your paying customers — message each one today and book a specific time this week to hear their experience.
Reach out to every paying customer and book a 10-minute feedback call for this week — tell them you want to hear what's working and what's not.
Contact all your current customers today and schedule brief calls: 'I want to understand your experience with the product — 10 minutes this week?'
tasks.why: Your first customers' experience determines whether they refer others or quietly churn.

variants: Check Stripe for any new payments from your follow-up email — log the total and calculate your revenue to date.
Open Stripe, count new payments since yesterday's email, and write your total revenue number — how close are you to 5 paying users?
Log your post-follow-up sales: check Stripe payment count, calculate total revenue, and note whether you've hit 5 paying customers yet.
tasks.why: Revenue tracking daily keeps you honest about whether your messaging is converting.

variants: Conduct or prepare for the first customer feedback call — if the call is scheduled, write 5 questions; if it already happened, write your 3 key takeaways.
Prepare for your customer feedback call: write 5 questions about how they use the product, what they love, and what's frustrating — have them ready before the call.
Either run your first customer feedback call today or prepare your question list: 5 open-ended questions that reveal how customers actually use the product.
tasks.why: Customer feedback calls at this stage reveal the real use case — which is often different from your assumption.

variants: Ship the most impactful improvement your customers have requested — code it, deploy, and message your paying customers that it's live.
Build the top customer-requested feature from your feedback calls: code the improvement, push it to production, and notify customers in a direct message.
Implement the highest-voted customer request today — even a small improvement shows responsiveness — deploy it and send a personal 'it's live' message to the customers who asked.
tasks.why: Shipping customer requests within days of launch creates the stickiness that turns users into advocates.

variants: Ask your most satisfied customer for a testimonial — send them a specific message: 'In one or two sentences, what would you tell someone who's considering this product?'
Request a testimonial from your happiest customer right now: message them and ask for a 1-2 sentence quote about the product — tell them you'll use it on the landing page.
Reach out to your best customer and ask for a quote: 'What would you say to someone who's on the fence about trying this?' — tell them you need their exact words.
tasks.why: A real customer quote on your landing page converts better than any copy you write yourself.

variants: If you haven't hit 5 paying customers yet, send personal DMs to 5 people on your waitlist who never bought — ask what specific concern is holding them back.
Identify 5 non-buyer subscribers and send each one a personal DM asking: 'You signed up but haven't bought yet — what's the main thing holding you back?'
Reach out to 5 waitlist subscribers who haven't purchased yet: ask each one directly what would need to change for them to try the product.
tasks.why: Direct objection research from non-buyers is as valuable as praise from buyers.

variants: Write your first customer case study draft: describe the customer's problem before, what they did with your product, and the specific outcome they got — 3 short paragraphs.
Draft case study #1: describe your first paying customer's situation, how they used the product, and the result they got — keep it under 200 words.
Write a short case study about your first customer: their problem, their action with your product, and their outcome — use their real words if they gave you a testimonial.
tasks.why: Case studies convert better than feature descriptions — they show the product in context.

variants: Add your first customer testimonial to your landing page — paste the quote, the customer's name, and their role or context.
Update your landing page with the testimonial you collected: add the customer's quote, their name, and a one-line description of who they are.
Put the testimonial live on your landing page right now: quote, name, context — social proof increases conversion immediately.
tasks.why: Social proof from real customers is the single highest-converting landing page element.

variants: Review your non-buyer objections from yesterday's DMs and identify the most common one — write a one-sentence rebuttal and add it to your landing page copy.
Take the top objection from your non-buyer conversations and add it as an FAQ on your landing page with a clear, honest answer.
Add an FAQ section to your landing page that addresses the objection you heard most from non-buyers — a direct, honest answer in 1-2 sentences.
tasks.why: Addressing objections on your landing page removes the last barrier before someone buys.

variants: Complete 2 more customer feedback calls or review notes from completed calls — add 3 new insights to your Beta Feedback doc.
Conduct your next customer feedback call today, then add the top 3 things you learned to your feedback log.
Do a customer feedback call right now or review notes from one completed this week — log 3 specific takeaways in your feedback doc.
tasks.why: Regular customer conversations keep you building the right things instead of what you imagine they want.

variants: Check your Stripe total and calculate your MRR or total revenue — write the number and how far you are from your 5-paying-user goal.
Open Stripe, count paying customers, calculate total revenue, and note the gap to your day-60 goal of 5 paying users.
Log today's sales numbers: total customers, total revenue, and the specific actions you'll take this week to close the gap to 5 paying users.
tasks.why: Revenue transparency with yourself creates urgency — knowing the gap is the only way to close it.

variants: Ship a meaningful product update — one new feature or significant improvement based on customer feedback — deploy it and announce it to your customers.
Deploy your weekly product update: implement the most-requested improvement, push it live, and send a message to all paying customers announcing the change.
Build and ship one meaningful improvement this week: something customers asked for, something that makes the core action faster or easier — deploy and announce it.
tasks.why: Weekly shipping signals to customers that you're actively building — that alone reduces churn.

variants: Share a build update on Twitter/X — a 3-tweet thread on what you shipped this week, what customer feedback drove it, and what's next — link to your product.
Post a weekly ship update on Twitter/X: tweet 1 is what you built, tweet 2 is the customer problem it solves, tweet 3 is your product link and an invitation to try it.
Write a Twitter/X thread for your weekly update: what you shipped, why you shipped it (customer quote), and a direct link to the product — post it today.
tasks.why: Consistent public build updates build an audience before you need one.

variants: Respond to every comment or reply on your Twitter/X thread from yesterday — engagement multiplies reach.
Check your Twitter/X thread from yesterday and reply to every comment — even a one-sentence reply extends the reach of the post.
Reply to all replies on your Twitter/X ship update: thank people who shared, answer questions, engage with criticism — every reply is a conversation that can lead to a customer.
tasks.why: Responding to comments is free marketing — each reply surfaces the post to a new audience.

variants: Write your second customer case study draft: a different customer's problem, their usage of your product, and the result — keep it under 200 words.
Draft case study #2 today: a second customer's before-state, what they did with the product, and the outcome — pull from a feedback call or conversation.
Write your second 200-word case study using a different customer's story — get their exact words from your feedback log if possible.
tasks.why: Two case studies give potential customers multiple identifiable personas to see themselves in.

variants: Add both case studies to your landing page — under a 'How it works for real users' section — and link them below your testimonial quote.
Update your landing page with both customer case studies: add them below the testimonials section so visitors see real outcomes before they hit the CTA.
Publish your 2 case study summaries on the landing page — a dedicated section between the benefits and the CTA — with real customer names and specifics.
tasks.why: Case studies on the landing page reduce buyer anxiety by showing the product in action.

variants: Post on IndieHackers today about your launch week results — share your sales number, your top lesson, and what you're building next.
Publish a launch recap post on IndieHackers: total sales, what worked, what flopped, and your next product priority — include the numbers honestly.
Write an IndieHackers update with real numbers from your launch: customers, revenue, email metrics, and the top 2 things you'd do differently.
tasks.why: Honest launch recaps attract more users than bragging posts — builders respect transparency.

variants: If you're not at 5 paying users, identify 3 more people from your waitlist to personally outreach to today — send a DM that directly addresses the top objection you've heard.
Send 3 targeted DMs to non-buyer waitlist members — address the top objection in your opening line and offer to answer one specific question.
Pick 3 unconverted waitlist subscribers and send each a personal message that opens by naming their likely objection and offering a direct answer.
tasks.why: Personalized objection-handling DMs convert better than any broadcast email.

variants: Ship your next product improvement — pick the second-highest customer request from your feedback log, build it, deploy it, and notify customers.
Build and deploy improvement #2 from your customer feedback list — message customers when it's live: 'You asked for this, it's in.'
Implement the second-most-requested customer improvement today: code it, commit it, push it to production, and send a personal note to the customer who requested it.
tasks.why: Weekly feature releases keep customers engaged and create content for your social updates.

variants: Do your week-8 review: paying customers to date, total revenue, top customer feedback theme, and 1 product decision it should drive.
Week-8 checkpoint: log paying users, MRR or total revenue, the #1 customer request you've implemented, and the #1 still-open request.
Review week 8 in your notes: sales count, revenue, top customer request you shipped, and what's next on the product roadmap — write all four.
tasks.why: Weekly reviews at this stage integrate sales, product, and customer feedback into one coherent picture.

variants: Write a 3rd customer case study — focus on a different use case or persona than the first two — and add it to your landing page.
Draft and publish case study #3: a different customer, a different starting point, a different outcome — the more variety, the more buyers see themselves in it.
Complete your third case study and add it to the landing page — make sure this one features a different persona or use case than your first two.
tasks.why: Three case studies on the landing page signals a proven product, not just an experiment.

variants: Decide your growth channel for days 57-90: paid ads, SEO, or founder-led sales — write your reasoning in one paragraph and your first action for the chosen channel.
Choose your Phase 3 growth strategy right now: ads, SEO, or direct founder sales — write in one paragraph why you chose it and what the first concrete step is.
Commit to your growth channel for the final 30 days: write 'I am doing [X] because [evidence]' and list 3 specific actions you'll take this week to start.
tasks.why: Choosing one growth channel and going deep beats spreading effort across three.

variants: Check Stripe — log total paying customers and total revenue today.
Open Stripe and note your current customer count and revenue number — write both in your notes.
Log today's Stripe snapshot: number of paying customers, total revenue, and your gap to any revenue goal you've set.
tasks.why: Daily revenue tracking in launch phase keeps you calibrated to what's actually working.

variants: Execute step 1 of your Phase 3 growth channel plan — if SEO, write your first target keyword and create the page; if ads, set up your first ad creative; if founder sales, DM 5 new contacts.
Start your chosen growth channel today with one concrete action: first keyword article draft, first ad copy, or first 5 outreach DMs to new prospects.
Launch your Phase 3 growth effort right now: first blog post draft, first ad creative, or first 5 cold DMs — pick the channel you committed to and take the first step.
tasks.why: Growth channels take time to compound — starting on day 57 means results by day 90.

variants: Conduct your next customer feedback call and log 3 specific insights — what did they say about their favorite feature and their biggest frustration?
Do a customer feedback call today and extract 3 specific things: what they use most, what they wish existed, and what they'd tell a friend about the product.
Run a customer interview right now and add 3 takeaways to your feedback log: their favorite part of the product, their biggest complaint, and their most-wanted improvement.
tasks.why: Regular customer calls this late in the process reveal what to prioritize for growth.

variants: Send your third growth follow-up to non-buyers on your waitlist — this time, lead with a case study headline and link to the full story on your landing page.
Write and send a third email to non-purchasing subscribers: open with 'Here's how [customer name] used this to [outcome]' and link to your case study.
Send email #3 to your non-buyer list: headline is a customer result, body is a 3-sentence case study summary, CTA is the Stripe link — send it today.
tasks.why: Third-touch emails with social proof hit a different audience segment than your first two.

variants: Continue Phase 3 growth channel execution: if SEO, write 300 more words on your first article; if ads, set up targeting and budget; if founder sales, follow up with your 5 new contacts.
Execute your next Phase 3 growth action: write more content, finalize your first ad, or send follow-ups to your new outreach contacts from yesterday.
Move to step 2 of your growth channel plan today — build on yesterday's start and get one deliverable closer to live.
tasks.why: Daily growth channel execution compounds — missing a day resets the momentum.

variants: Ship your weekly product update: implement improvement #3 from your customer feedback list, deploy it, and announce it to customers in a direct message.
Deploy your weekly feature this week: the third improvement from customer requests — ship it, announce it, and log it in your product changelog.
Build and ship this week's product update: fix or add the third thing customers asked for — deploy to Vercel and message your user base when it's live.
tasks.why: Consistent weekly shipping is your competitive moat against slower, more corporate competitors.

variants: Log your Stripe totals for the day — customers, revenue, and how close you are to the 5-paying-user goal.
Check Stripe and record today's customer count and revenue — note whether you've hit 5 paying users.
Open Stripe, count customers and revenue, and write both numbers — if you're at 5, mark the milestone.
tasks.why: The day before your 60-day milestone is the last chance to make a push if you're short.

variants: If you haven't hit 5 paying customers, DM 3 warm leads right now and offer them a 24-hour discount or a personal onboarding call to get them over the line.
Short of 5 customers? Send 3 direct messages today with a specific offer: personal onboarding, extra feature access, or a discounted price — make it time-sensitive.
If you're under 5 paying users, send 3 personalized DMs offering a reason to buy today: a time-limited deal, a personal setup call, or early access to a new feature.
tasks.why: A specific reason to act today converts better than a generic invitation to buy whenever.

variants: Write a Phase 2 summary: total customers, total revenue, top feature you shipped, top customer quote, and the biggest lesson from building and launching your MVP.
Draft your 60-day summary document: who bought, how much they paid, what feedback shaped the build, and the single most important thing you learned about your market.
Create a Phase 2 recap in your notes: list every paying customer, the total revenue, the 3 product improvements you shipped, and your biggest surprise from the launch.
tasks.why: A written Phase 2 recap makes your Phase 3 strategy sharper and more intentional.

variants: Do your 60-day milestone check: count paying customers, total revenue, and confirm whether you hit your 5-paying-user goal — write the honest result.
Mark your 60-day checkpoint: how many paying customers, how much revenue, did you hit 5 users — write the result and one sentence on what you're most proud of.
Log your 60-day milestone: final customer count, total revenue to date, goal achieved or not — write the number, the result, and your honest reaction.
tasks.why: 60 days in is a real milestone — acknowledge the progress, whatever the number is.

variants: Write 3 things you learned about your customers in Phase 2 that you didn't know in Phase 1 — then write how each one changes your product or messaging strategy going forward.
List 3 specific insights from your paying customers that surprised you — for each one, write what you'll change in Phase 3 because of it.
Document 3 customer discoveries from Phase 2: what did users actually do with the product vs. what you expected? Write what each discovery means for your Phase 3 strategy.
tasks.why: Explicit learning documentation converts experience into strategy — without it, you repeat the same mistakes.

variants: Write your Phase 3 goal for days 61-90: define your growth channel, your weekly publishing or outreach cadence, and your 90-day customer target — commit to specific numbers.
Set your Phase 3 targets in writing: growth channel chosen, weekly action cadence, and your target customer count by day 90 — write specific numbers, not ranges.
Define Phase 3 in one document: your growth method, your weekly ship cadence, your customer goal for day 90, and the one metric you'll track daily — write it all now.
tasks.why: Written Phase 3 targets with a chosen growth channel prevent the aimless 'posting and hoping' that kills most indie products.

variants: Open your Phase 3 plan from day 60 and execute action #1 right now — whatever you wrote as your first step, do it before anything else today.
Read your Phase 3 targets from yesterday and start with the very first action item — don't replan, just execute.
Pull up your day-60 Phase 3 goal document and run the first task on the list — Phase 3 starts right now.
tasks.why: Momentum from a milestone only lasts if you act on it immediately.

variants: Schedule a feedback call with your 3 most active customers this week — send them a Calendly link or a direct time proposal right now.
Book 3 customer interview calls for this week: message each of your most active users and propose a specific 10-minute slot.
Reach out to 3 paying customers today and schedule feedback calls — offer 2-3 specific time options so scheduling doesn't drag into next week.
tasks.why: Ongoing customer conversations in Phase 3 shape what you ship and how you grow.

variants: Write your first Phase 3 content piece: a 400-word blog post, a Twitter thread, or an IndieHackers story — pick the one that aligns with your growth channel and publish it today.
Publish your first Phase 3 content today: a short post, thread, or article that addresses your target user's problem using your product as part of the solution.
Create and publish one piece of content for your chosen growth channel: SEO article draft, founder Twitter thread, or IndieHackers story — ship it today.
tasks.why: Consistent content creation starts compounding in 4-6 weeks — the clock starts when you publish.

variants: Check Stripe right now and log today's customer count — are you adding new customers at a pace that will hit your Phase 3 goal?
Open Stripe, count customers, and calculate whether your current growth rate gets you to your day-90 target.
Log today's Stripe snapshot and check: at your current rate, when do you hit your Phase 3 customer goal? Write the projected date.
tasks.why: Projecting your arrival date to goal from current rate keeps you honest about whether growth tactics are working.

variants: Continue your Phase 3 growth channel: publish a second piece, send the next outreach batch, or set your first ad live — move the campaign one step forward.
Execute the next step in your Phase 3 growth plan: write the next post, send the next DMs, or finalize your first ad creative.
Move your Phase 3 growth channel effort forward by one concrete step today — whatever the next action is, do it now.
tasks.why: Growth channels reward consistency over intensity — one action per day beats one big burst.

variants: Conduct your first Phase 3 customer feedback call and write 3 specific insights from it — what do they use most, what do they want next, and what would make them refer a friend?
Run a customer feedback call today and extract 3 actionable insights: their primary use case, their most-wanted feature, and their referral trigger.
Do your first Phase 3 customer interview and log 3 takeaways: what's their most valuable use of the product, what's still frustrating them, and have they told anyone else about it?
tasks.why: Phase 3 customer conversations tell you how to grow — they reveal your referral triggers and growth levers.

variants: Do your week-9 review: total customers, total revenue, Phase 3 growth actions taken so far, and the single metric you're watching most closely.
Week-9 checkpoint: log paying users, revenue, Phase 3 actions completed, and the one number that tells you whether your growth channel is working.
Review week 9: customer count, revenue, growth channel actions executed, and your key leading indicator — write all four in your notes.
tasks.why: Phase 3 requires a different review lens — you're now tracking growth metrics, not just build metrics.

variants: Ship your weekly product update: one meaningful improvement from customer feedback, deployed and announced to all paying customers.
Deploy this week's product improvement: build it, push it to Vercel, and send all paying customers a message that it's live.
Build and ship your weekly improvement: pick the top unaddressed customer request, code it, deploy it, and announce it to your users.
tasks.why: Weekly shipping is now your product's heartbeat — missing a week signals stagnation to customers.

variants: Review your day-60 Phase 3 goal document and check: are the tactics you've executed so far on track to hit your day-90 customer target? Write your honest assessment.
Audit your Phase 3 execution: compare tactics planned to tactics executed, and project your customer count at day 90 based on current weekly growth — is the plan working?
Check in on your Phase 3 plan: what's been executed, what's falling behind, and based on current trajectory, will you hit your day-90 goal — write an honest yes or no.
tasks.why: Checking plan against reality at week 9 leaves enough time to adjust before day 90.

variants: Post on Twitter/X today — a 1-tweet insight about your target user's problem, no product pitch, just useful signal that builds your audience.
Tweet one genuinely useful insight about the problem you solve today — no sales angle, just value for your target audience.
Write and post a single tweet on your target user's problem: a counterintuitive insight, a common mistake, or a little-known solution — nothing about your product.
tasks.why: Audience-building tweets with no pitch perform better than product announcements — trust compounds.

variants: Execute the next Phase 3 growth action: if founder-led sales, send 5 new outreach DMs; if SEO, publish or draft another article; if ads, optimize your current campaign.
Move your growth channel forward today: 5 new DMs, a published article, or an ad optimization — one concrete step forward.

variants: Continue your Phase 3 growth work: write 300 words on your next article, send 5 new DMs, or review your ad performance and adjust the copy.

Create a 3-tweet thread about what real users taught you about the problem — frame it as discoveries, not product features — post it today.

tasks.why: Every day's Stripe check in the final stretch tells you whether to push harder or celebrate.

variants: Reply to every response from yesterday's closing messages — move every interested prospect toward a purchase decision today.
Check your DMs for replies to yesterday's close and respond to each one within minutes — you have 3 days left, urgency is your advantage.
Respond immediately to every reply from yesterday's closing outreach — be direct and helpful, make it easy to say yes.
tasks.why: Fast response to buying signals in the final days converts more than any tactic.

variants: Begin writing your 90-day retrospective: what you set out to do, what you actually built, who your customers are, and what you learned — you'll share this on day 90.
Draft your 90-day recap document: the original goal, the reality of what got built, your customer count and revenue, and 5 specific lessons from the process.
Start your 90-day retrospective post: open with your original goal, then cover what you built, who bought it, and the 3 most important things you'd tell your day-1 self.
tasks.why: Writing the retrospective before day 90 means it's thoughtful, not rushed.

variants: Log Stripe one more time — count customers, revenue, and write the gap to goal with 2 days remaining.
Open Stripe, count total customers, and write: 'X customers, Y revenue, Z more needed in 2 days' — be specific.
Check Stripe and write your exact status: customers, revenue, remaining gap — 2 days left.
tasks.why: Two days left — knowing the exact number drives the right actions today.

variants: Do one final targeted outreach push: message 5 more prospects using your highest-converting approach — these are your last cold contacts before day 90.
Send 5 final outreach messages today: best template, best targets, clean send — your last cold outreach of the 90-day sprint.
Execute your last cold outreach batch: 5 DMs, proven template, new contacts — after today, focus shifts to closing existing conversations.
tasks.why: Five last outreach messages can still produce paying customers before day 90.

variants: Finish your 90-day retrospective draft — you'll publish it tomorrow or on day 90 — add your final customer count, revenue, and top lesson.
Complete your retrospective document today: final stats, key lessons, and what comes next — you'll share it on day 90.
Finalize your day-90 recap post: add your current stats, complete the lessons section, and write a forward-looking final paragraph — ready to publish tomorrow.
tasks.why: A finished retrospective is your best marketing piece — it shows the journey, which attracts the next wave of users.

variants: Close every open outreach conversation today: follow up with everyone you messaged in the past 5 days who hasn't replied — a final nudge before day 90.
Send one last follow-up to every unanswered outreach message from days 84-88 — final nudge before the close of your sprint.
Clear your outreach backlog: send a final follow-up to every prospect who received a message in the last 5 days but hasn't responded.
tasks.why: The last follow-up on day 89 converts the people who were almost ready to buy.

variants: Send the 90-day founder letter you drafted on day 84 to your entire customer list right now — don't wait for day 90.
Send your customer letter today: open your day-84 draft, review it once, and send it to every paying customer — they deserve to hear from you before the sprint ends.
Deliver your founder letter to customers today: the thank-you, the summary of what you built, and your vision for what's next — send it before day 90 so it doesn't feel rushed.
tasks.why: A personal letter from the founder to customers deepens loyalty and triggers word-of-mouth sharing.

variants: Check Stripe one final time before day 90 and log your near-final customer count and revenue — note any last-day purchases that come in tonight.
Take your day-89 Stripe snapshot: total customers, total revenue, and your official progress toward your 90-day goal — tomorrow is the final count.
Log your penultimate Stripe check: customers, revenue, and write tomorrow's agenda for your final 90-day milestone review.
tasks.why: Day 89 gives you the clearest view of what your 90-day checkpoint will show — no surprises tomorrow.

variants: Open Stripe right now and record your final 90-day numbers: total paying customers, total revenue, and whether you hit your Phase 3 goal — write all three in your notes.
Count your final 90-day Stripe stats: every paying customer, your total revenue, and a clear yes or no on your customer goal — write the honest numbers.
Do your 90-day final Stripe audit: paying customers, revenue, goal achieved or not — write the exact numbers and your honest reaction to what you built.
tasks.why: Day 90 is your report card — the numbers don't lie and they set the baseline for everything that comes next.

variants: Publish your 90-day retrospective on IndieHackers today: the original goal, the validation process, the launch, the customer count, the revenue, and your 5 biggest lessons — post it with your product link.
Post your complete 90-day SaaS journey on IndieHackers right now: validation, building, launching, growing — include your real numbers and the link to your product.
Publish your 90-day retrospective publicly: what you set out to do, what you actually shipped, your real customer and revenue numbers, and what you'd do differently — include the product link.
tasks.why: A transparent 90-day post on IndieHackers is one of the most powerful marketing pieces a founder can publish — it attracts users, partners, and community.

variants: Write your next 90-day plan: based on everything you've built and learned, define your goals for customers, revenue, and product improvements for the next quarter — commit to the numbers.
Set your next 90-day targets today: customer goal, revenue goal, one new growth channel to test, and the top 3 product improvements you'll prioritize — write them with specific numbers.

Plan your next 90 days right now: you've validated, built, launched, and grown a real SaaS — where are you taking it? Write your customer target, revenue target, and the one thing that will make or break the next quarter.
tasks.why: Ending day 90 with a written next-90-day plan ensures momentum doesn't die at the finish line — it becomes the new starting line.
